did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture with 26 layers, optimized via the Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO) metaheuristic algorithm, to perform multi-class skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work introducing a novel framework called 'HHOForSkin' that combines a custom 26-layer CNN with Harris Hawk Optimization for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology, datasets (HAM10000), and reports new empirical results (99.1% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Efficient Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO)-Based Framework for Accurate Skin Cancer Prediction
brief_summary: This study introduces HHOForSkin, a novel framework combining a custom 26-layer Convolutional Neural Network with the Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO) metaheuristic algorithm for multi-class skin cancer classification. The model was trained and evaluated on the publicly available HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,500 dermoscopy images across seven diagnostic categories. The HHO algorithm was utilized to automatically fine-tune the CNN's hyperparameters and features to address the challenges of manual architecture design. Performance evaluation demonstrated that the proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 99.1% and an F1 score of 98.93%. These results position the framework as a high-performing solution for seven-class skin cancer classification compared to related works.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin cancer (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin cancer images using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for seven classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin cancer dataset comprising dermoscopy images of pigmented lesions, sourced from regularly updated sources and the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains images of a single lesion acquired at different times and under different lighting conditions.
classes_used: akiec (actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease)

use.training: Used for training the HHOForSkin framework after data balancing via RandomOverSampler, resulting in 46,935 balanced images (6,705 per class).
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance (accuracy and F1 score).
use.other: Data augmentation techniques (translational, specular, and rotational) were applied to the original dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

HAM10000.notes: Table 7 compares HHOForSkin against other works. HHOForSkin (99.1%) is compared against models such as EfficientNetV2S (99.1%), DNN (99.7% on ISIC archive), and various others on different datasets or subsets.

architecture_details.optimization_method: Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO) metaheuristic algorithm used to automatically fine-tune hyperparameters and features
architecture_details.hyperparameters_optimized: 10-dimensional vector representing hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, batch size, etc.)

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but HHO was used to optimize hyperparameters including learning rate
training_details.learning_rate: 0.001 (base setting for HHO), reduced by factor of 0.5 via ReduceLROnPlateau callback when plateau detected, minimum learning rate 0.00001
training_details.epochs: 8 epochs reported for non-optimized model; HHO optimization ran for MaxIter=2 iterations (with 451 iterations per epoch for unbalanced dataset and 2113 for balanced dataset in convergence analysis)

data_split.unbalanced: Training: 70% (7210 images), Validation: 10% (802 images), Testing: 20% (2003 images)
data_split.balanced: Training: 70% (33,793 images), Validation: 10% (3755 images), Testing: 20% (9387 images)

training_details.data_balancing: RandomOverSampler used to balance the dataset

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: HHOForSkin (Custom 26-layer CNN optimized with Harris Hawk Optimization)
preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing/scaling to uniform sizes (tested 256x256, 128x128, and 64x64; final selected size 128x128)
Data balancing using RandomOverSampler to address class imbalance (resulting in 46,935 balanced images)
Data augmentation techniques: translational, specular, and rotational transformations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to each class in the HAM10000 dataset to address variability in data.

Used to address class imbalance by duplicating instances from minority classes randomly until all seven classes had 6,705 images each, resulting in a total of 46,935 balanced images.

conclusions: The proposed HHOForSkin framework, which combines a custom 26-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO), achieved an average accuracy of 99.1% and an F1 score of 98.93% on the HAM10000 dataset for seven-class skin cancer classification.
The HHO metaheuristic algorithm effectively automates the fine-tuning of CNN hyperparameters and features, addressing the challenges and time consumption associated with manual architecture design.
The HHO-optimized model significantly outperformed the non-optimized CNN variant, which achieved 93.2% accuracy on the balanced dataset, demonstrating the benefit of automated optimization.

The proposed method achieved performance comparable to or higher than state-of-the-art models such as EfficientNetV2S (99.1%) and DNN (99.7% on ISIC archive), positioning it as a high-performing solution for multi-class skin cancer detection.
Data balancing using RandomOverSampler was critical for performance, as the non-optimized model on the imbalanced dataset achieved only 71.4% accuracy compared to 93.2% on the balanced dataset.

The study acknowledges that manual feature selection in traditional Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems can lead to decreased accuracy due to limited feature sets and reliance on human intuition, which the proposed deep learning approach mitigates by automatically learning hierarchical representations.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6,705 nevi vs. 115 dermatofibromas) and potential selection bias from specific dermatology clinics, limiting generalizability to diverse populations.

Data balancing was achieved using RandomOverSampler, which duplicates minority class samples; this technique can lead to overfitting and does not address underlying distributional biases in the original data.

The paper acknowledges that traditional CAD systems suffer from limited feature sets due to manual selection, but does not provide external validation on independent datasets to confirm the robustness of the HHO-optimized CNN beyond the HAM10000 subset.

Performance metrics (99.1% accuracy) are reported on a single dataset split; the lack of cross-validation or external test sets raises concerns about data leakage risks or over-optimistic performance estimates.
The authors note that while HHO automates hyperparameter tuning, the computational complexity of metaheuristic optimization may limit scalability in resource-constrained clinical environments.

The study compares the proposed model against various state-of-the-art methods, but some comparisons are made against models trained on different datasets or subsets (e.g., DNN on ISIC archive), making direct performance comparisons potentially unfair or misleading.
